reason: Refutation fails on the merits. (1) No under-flagging of the real defect class: the rewrite keeps high for the planted in-loop session.commit() that breaks batch atomicity, and block_threshold stays 'high'; it only replaces an UNBOUNDED 'any risk is at least high' floor with a demonstrability gate, and the bad fixture (tests/eval/fixtures/data-integrity/bad/non-atomic-bulk-insert/change.patch) is a concrete demonstrable atomicity defect on a changed line that passes that gate. All three baseline bad-case repeats flag the L40 in-loop commit as high, so detection is unaffected. (2) The known-bad fixture cannot slip: nothing about the change touches the bad fixture or lowers severity for demonstrable atomicity breaks. (3) The good fixture is genuinely clean as a data-integrity matter (single atomic commit, name strip/length validation, order-preserving de-dup, 1000-row bound). The baseline over-flags it (fp 1.00) on two FALSE claims verified against the actual template: 'created_at=None after commit' — but src/framework_cli/template/src/{{package_name}}/db/engine.py sets expire_on_commit=False and models.py gives created_at server_default=func.now(), and the template's own test asserts created.created_at is not None; and 'missing de-dup invariant' — but items.name is String(255) NOT NULL with NO unique=True (only webhook event_id is UNIQUE), so de-dup is not a violated store invariant. The codebase-bar clause that suppresses these is therefore correct, not a loosening. (4) The fixture edits do not make the bad case ambiguous or the good case dirty: the good-fixture edit only realigns the 'no per-row refresh' docstring that BAITS the created_at=None FP (cosmetic, leaves data-integrity correctness intact), and the added second good fixture is additive FP coverage. The only residual concern — baking template-specific facts into the prompt could go stale on a future template change — is a maintenance note, not a reason to block: the facts are verified true today. SHIP.
